food_name: Cendol (Pandan Jelly, Coconut Milk and Gula Melaka Shaved Ice Dessert)
aliases: pandan rice flour jelly shaved ice

english: pandan rice flour jelly shaved ice

region_of_origin: Malay Archipelago — Southeast Asia; Penang, Malaysia widely regarded as the gold standard
food_subgroup: Shaved ice dessert with pandan rice flour jelly and coconut milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Cendol. Closest parent concept is shaved ice dessert with coconut milk and starch jelly (FOODON:03315595 — iced dessert food product). No exact URI available; null used honestly.
ontology.foodex2_description: Shaved ice dessert with pandan rice flour jelly, coconut milk, and palm sugar syrup (cendol style) — closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for cendol. A0CEL (iced dessert / shaved ice category) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for cendol or its direct parent category in the clinical food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of cendol as served at a hawker stall or café is approximately 350 g, comprising shaved ice, pandan jelly strands, coconut milk, and gula melaka syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Cendol — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore (Foodo); Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Malaysia (MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo for cendol as a composite dish, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for coconut milk and gula melaka component values. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Palm Sugar (Gula Melaka) and Composite Shaved Ice Desserts — University of Sydney GI Database; cross-referenced with peer-reviewed literature on palm sugar GI (GI ~35–55)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cendol exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) gula melaka (palm sugar) GI of approximately 35–55 as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Wolever et al.); (2) rice flour starch jelly GI analogous to rice-based starch products (~65–70); (3) coconut milk fat content moderating the overall glycaemic response. The composite GI of 60 (medium) is a defensible weighted estimate. Glycemic load of 30.5 calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 50.75) / 100 = 30.45, rounded to 30.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (35 mg/100 g); a standard 350 g bowl delivers only approximately 123 mg sodium, well below the 600 mg per-meal threshold.
hypertension.guidance: Cendol is suitable for individuals with hypertension from a sodium perspective; however, the high saturated fat from coconut milk (6.2 g/100 g, ~21.7 g per bowl) warrants moderation to protect long-term cardiovascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~30.5) driven by concentrated palm sugar syrup and rice flour jelly; a standard 350 g bowl delivers approximately 50.75 g carbohydrate and 36.75 g sugar.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit cendol to a half-bowl portion (approximately 175 g, GL ~15) at most, choose vendors using pure gula melaka (GI ~35–55) rather than cane sugar syrup, and avoid additional toppings such as red beans or extra syrup to minimise the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (6.2 g/100 g) from full-fat coconut milk; a standard 350 g bowl delivers approximately 21.7 g saturated fat, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 20 g in a single serving.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit cendol to a small portion (100–150 g) consumed infrequently, and where possible request a version made with light coconut milk to substantially reduce the saturated fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from full-fat coconut milk may promote systemic inflammation; a 350 g bowl delivers approximately 21.7 g saturated fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit cendol to a small portion (100–150 g) no more than once every two weeks, and prefer light coconut milk versions where available; the pandan jelly itself has no known pro-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cendol is one of Southeast Asia's most iconic and visually striking desserts, widely enjoyed across Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Thailand. The dish centres on bright green, worm-like jelly strands — made by extruding a cooked dough of rice flour (or mung bean flour) mixed with fresh pandan juice through a perforated mould into cold water — which are served over a mound of finely shaved ice. The assembled bowl is then generously drenched with rich, creamy coconut milk and a thick, dark syrup made from gula melaka (palm sugar), producing a layered combination of cool, silky, earthy-sweet, and lightly floral flavours. Optional toppings include cooked red beans (kacang merah), creamed corn, or durian for a premium variant. Penang cendol is widely regarded as the gold standard. Nutritionally, cendol is a moderate-energy dessert (118 kcal/100 g) dominated by fat from coconut milk (7.2 g/100 g, of which 6.2 g is saturated) and carbohydrate from palm sugar and rice flour jelly (14.5 g/100 g). A key distinguishing feature is the use of gula melaka, which has a lower glycaemic index (GI ~35–55) than refined cane sugar, giving cendol a more moderate overall GI (~60, medium) compared to many other Malay sweet beverages. However, the large standard serving size (350 g) results in a high glycaemic load (~30.5) and high saturated fat intake (~21.7 g), making it a high-concern food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Sodium is very low (35 mg/100 g), making it suitable for hypertension from a salt perspective. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal.
common_user_queries: cendol gula melaka vs cane sugar

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (primary approved source for Malaysian dishes). Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2. The food is a well-documented composite dish with consistent nutrient profiles across both databases. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for gula melaka and rice starch components, clearly documented.
notes: Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo; cendol contains no animal-derived fat components (coconut milk is plant-based). Vitamin A is 0.0 mcg RAE — confirmed zero; pandan colouring provides chlorophyll but negligible provitamin A. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dessert with no animal products. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero; no UV-exposed mushrooms or animal products present. Trans fat is 0.0 g — confirmed zero; no hydrogenated fats used. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; no direct cendol GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load of 30.5 is calculated for a 350 g standard serving. The portions array in the input contained three entries with two marked is_default=true at 350 g; the canonical default serving of 350 g (1 bowl) has been adopted. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is the HPB Singapore Foodo platform homepage; direct deep-link to the cendol entry was not available at time of processing.
